name: Ororbia, City of Pamplona – Circular Route
name_original: Ororbia, Ciudad de Pamplona – Ruta Circular
region_intro: Touring cycling around Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea offers diverse landscapes shaped by riverine paths and varied mountain terrain. The region is characterized by important rivers like the Arga and Arakil, which create fertile riverbanks and lush vegetation. Cyclists encounter sub-Mediterranean flora, including holm oaks and oaks, alongside geological features such as limestones and marls that contribute to the distinct relief. Mountains like Sarbil/Mortxe and the Oltza mountains provide varied topography and elevation changes for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea The most popular touring cycling route is Ororbia, City of Pamplona – Circular Route, a 23.3 miles (37.6 km) trail that takes 2 hours 41 minutes to complete. This moderate route follows riverine paths and offers a tranquil environment near Pamplona. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Etxauri, Alto del Perdón, Balsa Iiazabal – Circular Route, a difficult 25.5 miles (41.0 km) path. This route features significant elevation gain and views towards the Alto del Perdón, a landmark on the Way of St. James. Local touring cyclists also love the Etxauri Pass loop from Etxauri, a 62.6 miles (100.7 km) trail leading through varied mountain terrain, often completed in about 7 hours 5 minutes. Touring cycling around Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea is defined by riverine paths, mountain views, and sub-Mediterranean flora. The network offers options for different ability levels. The routes in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 350 reviews. More than 7500 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea?
A: There are over 300 touring cycling routes in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when touring cycling in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea?
A: The region offers diverse landscapes, from picturesque riverine paths along the Arga and Arakil rivers to varied mountain terrain with features like Sarbil/Mortxe and the Oltza mountains. You'll encounter lush vegetation, sub-Mediterranean flora including holm oaks and oaks, and geological formations of limestones and marls.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea?
A: Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea benefits from a transitional climate, influenced by both Atlantic and Mediterranean conditions. This generally provides a rich and dynamic natural backdrop that changes with the seasons. Spring and autumn often offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, while summer can be warm. Winter cycling is possible, but conditions may vary.

Q: Are there options for both easy and challenging touring cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area caters to all levels. You'll find over 30 easy routes, more than 110 moderate routes, and over 160 difficult routes, including those with significant elevation gain for experienced cyclists.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer views of natural features. You might encounter the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4817192" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;banks of the Arga River&lt;/a&gt;, or even discover waterfalls like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4013419" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Artazul Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5315216" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zokorena Dam&lt;/a&gt;. The region's sub-Mediterranean flora, including holm oaks and oaks, also provides a rich natural backdrop.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks accessible by bike?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. You can cycle past the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4817230" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magdalena Bridge&lt;/a&gt; in Pamplona, an important medieval bridge on the Camino de Santiago. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/654847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pilgrim Monument at Alto del Perdón&lt;/a&gt;, also on the Way of St. James, is another significant landmark offering historical context and scenic views.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Absolutely. Many routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e973141875/ororbia-city-of-pamplona-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ororbia, City of Pamplona – Circular Route&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a tranquil ride along riverine paths.

Q: Can I find routes suitable for families with children?
A: Yes, the western end of the Comarca de Pamplona Riverside Park extends into the area, offering gentle routes through towns like Arazuri, Ororbia, and Ibero. These paths provide a tranquil environment away from heavy traffic, making them suitable for family outings.

Q: Are there opportunities for gravel cycling or vineyard routes?
A: The region is recognized for its gravel rides, allowing cyclists to experience the 'rugged beauty' of the area. Additionally, you can find unique routes that run through local vineyards, offering a chance to combine cycling with wine tasting.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 350 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from lush riverbanks to varied mountain views, and the network of routes that cater to all ability levels.

Q: Are there any long-distance cycling paths connected to Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea?
A: Yes, Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea is situated near significant long-distance cycling paths. A segment of the EuroVelo 3, which is part of the Way of St. James, passes through the broader Pamplona region, offering extended touring possibilities.

Q: Where can I find parking or public transport access for touring cycling routes?
A: While specific parking details for every route are best checked on individual tour pages, the proximity to Pamplona and its Riverside Park suggests various access points. Public transport options in the broader Pamplona region can also facilitate reaching starting points for routes in Cendea de Olza/Oltza Zendea.

Q: Can I find challenging routes with significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. For those seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e968222978/etxauri-alto-del-perdon-balsa-iiazabal-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Etxauri, Alto del Perdón, Balsa Iiazabal – Circular Route&lt;/a&gt; feature significant elevation gain and offer rewarding views. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31876286" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Etxauri Pass loop from Etxauri&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 100 km with substantial climbing.
tips: The structure is 3 meters wide and more than 66 meters long. One of the longest in Spain.

This is the most important bridge of the four medieval ones that cross the Arga as it passes through Pamplona.

It was built in the twelfth century and later renovated in the nineteenth century. It consists of five large slightly pointed arches, triangular tajamares and semicircular discharge arches on the supports. It is related to that of Puente la Reina. A transept, probably built in the sixteenth century, points the traveler at one end of the bridge to the entrance of the Camino de Santiago to the city. In it you can see the image of the Jacobean saint.

After crossing this Romanesque bridge, the pilgrim arrives at the Tejería Park and from there to the walls of Pamplona, under the old city of Navarrería, which is accessed by the Portal de Francia.
https://www.pamplona.es/turismo/puente-de-la-magdalena#:~:text=Este%20es%20el%20puente%20m%C3%A1s,medio%20punto%20en%20los%20apoyos.

This cathedral is a beautiful medley of architectural styles. Its construction began in Romanesque style, later the floor plan was changed to the Gothic style, in which the cathedral was completed. Finally, the main portal was built in neoclassical style. Mary's bell, located in the left tower, dates from 1584 and is the second largest in Spain.

Located on one side of the Cathedral, this square invites you to relax, as it is a quiet and quiet place where you can usually hear only the sound of the water from the fountain. In addition, the shade of the trees (and the Cathedral itself) make it a cool place in summer.
Here is celebrated the mornings of the first non-festive Saturday of each month, the already traditional Rastro de las Buenas Pulgas, a market dedicated to the purchase and sale of antiques and second-hand objects, which has an important influx of buyers-sellers and curious, and in whose stalls you can find many curiosities and small wonders.
